summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final orders – Where the mother seeks sole decision-making responsibility for all major long term issues for the child – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer, supported by the father, seeks for the father to have sole decision-making responsibility for all major long term issues for the child – Where the mother seeks for the child to spend time with the father in accordance with the child’s wishes – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer, supported by the father, seeks for the child to live with the father and there be a moratorium on the child’s time with the mother – Where there are final orders by consent for the child’s brother to live with the mother and spend time with the father in accordance with the brother’s wishes – Where the mother is unable to cooperate with the father or support his relationship with the child – The father to have sole decision-making responsibility – The child to live with the father and live with the mother equally – Where it is in the child’s best interests to promote his relationship with both parents and his brother.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARTNETT J

INTRODUCTION

This matter was initiated in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) upon the applicant father (“the father”) filing an Initiating Application on 24 January 2024 seeking final parenting orders in respect of the children X born in 2009 (“[X]”) and Y born in 2012 (“[Y]”) (collectively, “the children”). The matter was listed for trial on 25 November 2025. On the morning of 25 November 2025, the matter was transferred by order made pursuant to s 149 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1). The trial then proceeded before me for four days, with the trial concluding on 28 November 2025.
On the first day of the trial, the mother was represented by Mr Murray of counsel, who was instructed by Ann Legal solicitors. The mother refused to attend the hearing that day. As a preliminary matter, I heard submissions from counsel for each of the father, the mother, and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) as to three subpoenas and an Application in a Proceeding, which the mother had unilaterally filed (not through her solicitors). Those documents are set out below.
On 30 September 2025, the mother subpoenaed E Pty Ltd T/A F Company (“the company”) seeking production of documents pertaining to Ms G, the father’s current partner. In addition to submissions from all parties’ counsel, I heard submissions from Ms H who had filed (on 28 October 2025) a Notice of Objection to the subpoena on behalf of the company. I made an order setting the subpoena aside (Order 1 of the orders made 25 November 2025). I observe that each of counsel for the mother, father and the ICL submitted that the subpoena was oppressive and that the documents sought were irrelevant to the matters to be determined by the Court. Further, the mother did not seek leave pursuant to rule 6.27(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) to issue such subpoena.
On 13 November 2025, the mother had subpoenaed Ms J to attend in person at the trial to give evidence. I ordered the setting aside of that subpoena (Order 2 of the orders made 25 November 2025), there being no objection to that course. The subpoena had not been served on any of the parties, and the mother had not sought leave pursuant to rule 6.27(1) of the Rules to issue such subpoena.
Further, the mother had, on 13 November 2025, filed a subpoena directed to Mr K, deputy principal of the L School (the school which the children attend) seeking the production of various documents as particularised by the mother in the subpoena. Mr K attended the Court and had in his possession the documents as set out in the subpoena. Counsel for each of the mother, the father, and the ICL did not object to the material being provided to the Court by Mr K, and I subsequently made an order granting leave for each of the parties to inspect that subpoenaed material (Order 2 of the first set of orders made 26 November 2025).
Finally, I heard submissions from counsel as to the Application in a Proceeding which the mother had filed on 21 November 2025 seeking orders that the ICL be discharged. Counsel for each of the parties opposed the making of such orders, including counsel for the mother. Counsel for the mother submitted that he could not formulate an argument as to why the ICL should be discharged. Counsel for the father submitted that it was “too late in the day” to make such an application. I made an order dismissing the Application in a Proceeding filed 21 November 2025 (Order 3 of the orders made 25 November 2025) on the basis that the application would not have been a successful application on the material before me, and because the mother had not attended to prosecute her application, nor had she provided instructions to her solicitors to prosecute the application.
On 25 November 2025, in the context of the trial proceeding, I also made the following orders:
...
The mother, [MS STEINAR] born [in 1978] attend in person in Courtroom 3, Level 4 of the Newcastle Registry at 9.00am (AEDT).
In the event the mother does not comply with Order 4 herein, the Court shall issue a warrant for her arrest and for her to be brought before the Court as soon as practicable.
Otherwise, all extant applications are adjourned to 26 November 2025 at 10.00am (AEDT).

(As per the original)
On 26 November 2025, being the second day of trial, the mother was in attendance from 9.00am, as required by the orders made 25 November 2025. As a further preliminary issue, Mr Murray of counsel made submissions on behalf of himself and his instructing solicitor as to the need for them both to withdraw from the proceeding. The mother wished for Mr Murray and Ann Legal to continue representing her but submitted that she had concerns about the way Ann Legal had prepared her case. The mother conceded that she “made strong accusations” of her solicitor “being untruthful”. Mr Murray of counsel submitted that he could no longer act for the mother, a submission he did not make lightly, including because he would not represent the mother without being instructed by a solicitor. I made orders granting Mr Murray and Ann Legal leave to withdraw. The mother became a litigant in person. She was unable to cross-examine the father due to the existence of an operative section 102NA of the Act order.
On 26 November 2025, final parenting orders were made by consent which provided for the elder child X’s living arrangements. X was aged 16 years at the time of the trial. Those orders are set out in paragraph 86 below. As a result of those orders, the child Y became the sole child (henceforth also referred to as “the child”) subject of these proceedings.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The father relied upon the following:
(1) his Amended Application for Final Orders filed 19 February 2025;

(2) his affidavit filed 6 November 2025;

(3) the affidavit of Ms G filed 28 November 2025;

(4) his Outline of Case filed 24 November 2025; and

(5) the Family Report of Court Child Expert Dr M dated 17 January 2025 (“the Family Report”).

The mother relied upon the following:
(1) her affidavit filed 26 November 2025;

(2) the affidavit of Ms N filed 27 October 2025; and

(3) her proposed minute of orders sought tendered on 27 November 2025.

The mother was granted leave, in the circumstances described hereafter, to rely upon paragraphs 26 to 51 inclusive of a further affidavit filed 9 February 2026 pursuant to orders made 17 March 2026.

The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) relied upon the following:
(1) the ICL’s Case Summary Document filed 18 November 2025;

(2) the ICL’s proposed minute of orders sought tendered on 28 November 2025; and

(3) the Family Report of Dr M.

ORDERS SOUGHT

The father adopted, and consented to, the orders sought in the proposed Minute of Order tendered into evidence by the ICL on 28 November 2025, as set out at paragraph 15 below.
The mother sought orders, relevantly, as follows:
That the child [Y], born [in 2012] (“[Y]”), live with the Mother.
That the mother have sole parental responsibility for all long-term major decisions concerning [Y], including but not limited to decisions regarding his:
a. Education and schooling;

b. Health and medical treatment;

c. Psychological and therapeutic support;

d. Religion and cultural upbringing;

e. Extracurricular activities.
That [Y] spend time with and communicate with the father only in accordance with [Y’s] own wishes, without pressure, coercion, or imposition from either parent or any third party.
Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act 1975, the father be restrained by injunction from:

a. Bringing [Y] into contact with [Ms O]; and

b. Permitting [Y] to communicate with [Ms O];
for a minimum period of three (3) years, or for such further period as the Court considers appropriate.
That the father be restrained by injunction from approaching, attending or attempting to attend:
a. The mother’s residence; and

b. The child’s school;
except as otherwise ordered by the Court.
That the child continue to engage with [D Program] for counselling and family therapy for as long as is recommended by the treating professionals involved in that program.
That the Court make any other orders it considers necessary to protect the safety and wellbeing of the mother and the child.
The ICL sought orders as follows:[1]
1. All previous parenting orders are discharged.
The child [Y] born [in 2012] will live with the father.
The father shall have sole responsibility for making decisions about long-term major issues in respect of [Y].
Subject to order 6 below, [Y] will spend no time and have no contact with the mother for a period of three months from the date of this order.
Thereafter and until such time as [Y] turns 16, [Y] will spend time with the mother as determined by the father noting that the father may require that such time is professionally supervised with the costs to be shared by the parties.
[Y] will communicate with the mother at times and in a manner determined by the father.
The father shall nominate the school to be attended by [Y] and he shall inform the mother of the name and contact details of the school.
The mother shall be at liberty to contact by telephone or email for the sole purpose of requesting that the school provide her with school reports, newsletters and the opportunity to purchase school photos at her own expense.
Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act 1975, for the personal protection of [Y], the mother be restrained by injunction from:
approaching or contacting [Y] otherwise than with the prior consent in writing from the father or in accordance with these orders;
causing or permitting [X] to approach or contact [Y] otherwise than with the prior consent in writing from the father;
approaching or contacting [Y’s] school, otherwise than with the prior consent in writing from the father.
The father is at liberty to provide a copy of these orders and reasons for judgment to the Principal of [Y’s] school and to any medical professional or registered psychologist [Y] attends.
The ICL shall provide a copy of these orders and reasons for judgement to the NSW department of Communities and Justice within 7 days of these orders.

RELEVANT FACTUAL FINDINGS

In 1978, the mother was born. At trial, she was 47 years of age. Prior to September 2022, the mother was self-employed, operating a business. Thereafter, the mother became a part-time support worker. At trial the mother continued to work part-time as a support worker and was also working in events. The mother has always resided in New South Wales (“NSW”).
In 1980, the father was born. At trial, he was 45 years of age. The father is employed full-time basis as a tradesperson. The father grew up in Queensland, and his parents and brother are still located there.
In or around 2001, the mother and father commenced their relationship.
In or around 2004, the mother and father began cohabitation.
In 2008, the mother and father married.
In 2009, X was born. At the time of X’s birth, the mother and father were living in a rental property at Suburb P. The mother and father both cared for X after his birth, with the parties rearranging their work hours such that the mother would care for X in the morning and early afternoon before she went to work, and the father would care for X in the evenings after he had finished work.
In 2012, Y was born. The mother was working fewer hours in her business, and she assumed the primary carer role for both of the children after Y’s birth. The father would assist the mother in the care of the children of an afternoon and evening, after he had finished work, and on weekends. Y was 13 years of age at the time of trial.
In or around 2014, the mother and the father moved to a rental property in Suburb Q. The mother set up a second business at the Suburb Q rental property. For the duration of the time that the mother and father resided in Suburb Q, the children attended R School.
In 2014, the father was declared bankrupt upon a creditor’s application by the Australian Taxation Office. The parties’ consequent financial pressures led to arguments between them.
Throughout 2015 and 2016, the mother and father continued to share responsibility for the care of the children wherever possible. The mother would drive the children to school, and the father would pick them up after school. The father would take the children to their respective sporting activities on weekends, and both parents would take the children to swimming classes in Suburb S. The children were engaged in other extracurricular activities which also included Rugby League, the playing of musical instruments and musical performances. The parents supported their children academically and across an array of activities as described.
In 2016 or 2017, the mother opened a third business in Suburb T. Approximately 18 to 24 months after the Suburb T business had been opened, the parties closed both the Suburb T and Suburb Q businesses, leaving the initial Suburb U business open.
In 2017 or 2018, the father began working on a project in Region V which required him to stay at Region V accommodation from Monday to Friday. The father took this job out of financial necessity. The father telephoned the children most nights and spent weekends in Sydney with the family. During this time, the mother had a nanny stay in the family home who assisted the mother with the care of the children.
In or around the beginning of 2019, the mother and father moved to Region W and took up occupation of rental premises as their home. At this time, the mother still owned and operated the Suburb U business, but she rarely travelled to the business as the day-to-day operations were largely carried out by employees of the business. The business was not profitable, causing further stresses within the household.
Upon the family moving to Region W, the children attended the Z School in Town AA. The mother generally ran the household and cared for the children. The father obtained full-time employment as a plumber and would leave the home around 5 am and return in the mid-afternoon. He would assist with the preparation of the school lunches for the children and would often prepare dinner for the children and himself, as the mother pursued a different diet. Both parties continued their support of the children in the children’s engagement in extracurricular activities, and both parties attended parent/teacher meetings and other events associated with the school. Both parents were actively involved in the care of the children and in attending to their physical, including medical, and academic needs.
In 2020, X began attending L School, and Y began attending BB School. Once Y was in Year 5, he also began attending L School. The children continue to attend this school. Y at trial was in Year 7. The school has become embroiled in the parenting dispute, the school being a change-over location, and the mother seeking to engage the school staff, inappropriately, in the parenting dispute between the parties, and promote to them her version of events.
In 2020, the father moved out of the former matrimonial home in Region W and moved to Sydney where he lived with one of his friends. The mother and the father commenced attendance upon a marriage counsellor. The father continued to pay the rental payments due on the former matrimonial home, and he contributed toward the payment of the household expenses. The father travelled to Region W regularly to spend time with the children. The parties continued with marriage counselling until the marriage counsellor terminated the sessions on the basis that the sessions were not proving fruitful between the parties.
In mid to late 2020, the father moved back into the former matrimonial home in Region W with the mother and the children. The mother and the father slept in separate bedrooms. They attempted a reconciliation.
In late 2020, the mother and the father had an argument about the parties’ relationship and their finances. The children were not present in the room. The mother commenced to yell at the father. The father became agitated and punched his computer screen in frustration and likely anger, and injured his hand. The mother drove the father to the hospital whilst the maternal grandparents cared for the children.
In early 2021, the mother told the father that she thought they should separate. The father commenced looking for alternate accommodation for himself. The parties nevertheless continued to reside under the one roof.
In December 2021, the mother attended City B Police Station and requested to speak with police regarding alleged domestic violence as perpetrated by the father against her. The mother was taken to an interview room where she requested that an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order (“ADVO”) be taken out against the father for the mother’s protection. The mother told the police that the father exhibited emotional and controlling behaviour and would not let her access money. The mother also told the police of the incident that occurred 12 months earlier wherein the father had injured his hand. The mother further alleged that in December 2021, following a verbal altercation between the parties, the father had wrestled her and the mother “took 3 blows to the head so she bit him hard on the arm” but “was unable to go into any further details”.[2] The mother refused to provide the police with a statement. The mother expressed that she had ongoing fears that the father would continue to verbally abuse her. The mother played the police video recordings from her phone, and the police noted that both parties had thrown and smashed objects, and each was swearing at, and abusive to, the other.
In December 2021, NSW Police attended the former matrimonial home to caution the father and to ask him about the allegations the mother had made to the police in December 2021. The father admitted that he had a physical altercation with the mother in December 2021 but made no admissions as to the particulars of that altercation. The provisional ADVO issued against the father at that time did not require the father to vacate the former matrimonial home. At the time of NSW Police’s attendance, the father was alone with Y. NSW Police conducted a welfare check on Y. There were no concerns.
In or around late 2021, the father contacted DD Family Services to arrange a mediation with the mother. The mother refused to attend the mediation. The father obtained a certificate pursuant to s 60I of the Act. The mother and the father continued to live separated under the one roof and continued to share in the care of the children.
On 4 August 2022, the mother returned to the former matrimonial home at approximately 9.30pm. The father was under the influence of alcohol, having consumed several beers in the mother’s absence. The father engaged in an argument with the mother, during which the father refused to leave the home and reside elsewhere; and threatened to “punch” the mother’s “head in” and “burn the mother’s parents’ house down and kill them”.[3] The mother recorded the conversation on her mobile phone.
In August 2022, the mother and the children tested positive for COVID-19. The father, in response, elected to sleep in his car which was parked on the street in front of the former matrimonial home. It is probable that he also chose to distance himself from the mother.
In August 2022, the father remained sleeping in his car in front of the former matrimonial home. The mother called NSW Police and told them that the father had threatened her, and that he was sleeping in his car outside the home. NSW Police attended the home and saw the father asleep in his car in front of the house. Police woke the father, who told the police that he was sleeping there as the mother and the children were sick with COVID-19. The police spoke with the mother inside the house. The mother told the police that she was unsettled and fearful of the father and played to them the recording taken by her on her mobile phone of the father verbally threatening the mother and her parents on 4 August 2022. NSW Police arrested the father for domestic violence intimidation and escorted him to City B Police Station. The father told the police that he had no recollection of the events of 4 August 2022 and did not admit to the voices in the recording being that of himself and the mother. At trial, the father said in relation to the threats as made by him on 4 August 2022, that “he had said things that he regrets”.
In August 2022, NSW Police served the father with an interim ADVO with the following conditions:[4]
You must not do any of the following to [the mother], or anyone she has a domestic relationship with:
A) assault or threaten her,

B) stalk, harass or intimidate her, and
intentionally or recklessly destroy or damage any property or harm an animal that belongs to or is in the possession of [the mother]
...
You must not approach [the mother] or contact her in anyway, unless the contact is:
A) through a lawyer

B) to attend accredited or court-approved counselling, mediation and/or conciliation

C) as ordered by this or another court about contact with child/ren

D) as agreed in writing between you and the parent(s) about contact with child/ren...

...
7. You must not live at:
A) the same address as [the mother], or

B) [the former matrimonial home]
On 22 August 2022, the mother and father separated on a final basis. The father moved out of the former matrimonial home into his own rental accommodation in City B which is in relative proximity to the home of the mother and the children. Around this time, the children began spending between time with the father each week, on a flexible basis, and as agreed between the parties. That time included overnight time on alternate weekends and spending time together during the week on at least two occasions, with the father being engaged in the children’s sporting commitments and providing them with dinner. The parties had a co-operative arrangement that was beneficial to the children. The children’s time with their father proceeded as it had before separation, though not to the same extent given the separation. The children enjoyed their time with the father.
In September 2022, the father attended City B Local Court of NSW. The father pleaded guilty to the charge of intimidation domestic violence and consented to the ADVO being made on a final basis and for 2 years. The presiding magistrate made the ADVO on a final basis in the terms as particularised in paragraph 41 above.[5] The father was subject to a community release order.[6]
In or around September 2022, the mother began working as a support worker. The father would often care for the children on weekends when the mother worked. This care as provided by the father was in addition to his provision of care as described above.
In late 2022, Y fractured his wrist. Y required surgery to correct the fracture. The mother did not inform the father of Y’s injury until after the surgery, nor involve the father in the parental decisions necessary to be made in respect of Y’s injury and treatment.
Around December 2022, the father commenced a relationship with Ms G. Ms G is a qualified health professional and was aged 51 years at the time of trial. At trial, she was working on a part-time basis. Ms G is the mother of two daughters, one of whom is autistic and significantly disabled. Ms G’s children and the father get on well.
The father and Ms G do no live together and nor do they intend to commence cohabitation soon. Ms G would however be willing to help the father with any transport or medicals needs of the child Y, if he was to live with or spend time with the father. The mother considers that Ms G represents an ongoing risk of harm to the children. The mother has no evidentiary or other basis for forming that view. I find Ms G does not pose any risk to the child and further that she would support and assist the child when necessary and be entirely appropriate in her engagement with the child. That the mother concludes as she does and seeks orders restraining Ms G from being in the presence of the children is nothing more than a vindictive response intended to harm the father.
In February 2023, the mother followed Ms G into a restaurant and confronted her in what was not a pleasant exchange. The mother advised Ms G to “get as far away from him [the father] as she could”. The effect upon Ms G (of that episode) was that she told the father that she would no longer see him until he “got his affairs in order”. Such conflict was something Ms G wished to avoid. Further, she was not prepared to expose her children to any adverse behaviours surrounding the father.
Between March and May 2023, the mother perceived that there was an opportunity for the parties to reconcile and they spent some time together. They did not reconcile.
In August 2023, the father and Ms G resumed their relationship and at trial in November 2025, the relationship was continuing and happily so. Each provides support to the other, and although they do not live together, they spend considerable time with each other and with Ms G’s children.
The mother’s response to the father’s relationship with Ms G was to cease facilitating the children spending time with the father. The mother could not accommodate the father having another relationship post-separation, and in circumstances where she thought the parties may reconcile. That suspension of time between the children and the father, as instigated and enforced by the mother, has been detrimental to the advancement of the children’s best interests. It continued for over 12 months until the putting into effect of the orders made on 7 June 2024, as referred to hereafter. I note that the father had filed his Initiating Application in January 2024.
In or around August 2023, the mother again confronted Ms G, travelling to do so outside the father’s residence. I observe that the final ADVO was still in operation. The mother and Ms G had a verbal altercation which resulted in Ms G calling NSW Police. During that altercation, the mother told Ms G that the father had a mental health diagnosis.
In October 2023, the father was charged with contravening the final ADVO made in September 2022.[7] The father had wanted to see the children and had gone to the mother’s home. He parked out the front of her home and contacted the mother by text and phone requesting to see the children. The mother refused. The father repeated his behaviours.
In December 2023, the father attended City B Local Court for the hearing with respect to his ADVO contravention in October 2023. The father was convicted and sentence to a conditional release order without conviction for a 12-month period.
On 23 May 2024, a Child Impact Report was completed by Court Child Expert Ms CC. The Child Impact Report raised concerns about both parties’ mental health. Relevantly, the report writer, Ms CC, said as follows:
The father stated that he has had some concerns for the mother’s mental health in the past but is not aware if this is an ongoing concern. He stated that he would only be concerned if the mother was prescribed medication for any mental health diagnosis and was not compliant with this medication.
As previously stated, the mother described seeking an assessment from a clinical psychologist for a possible diagnosis of C-PTSD that directly relates to the alleged family violence from the father. She also stated that she strongly suspects that she has adult ADHD that is exacerbated by her trauma symptoms. The mother stated that she not undertaken any formal diagnosis as she does not have the funds to pay for a full assessment. She further reported that she has been engaged with a psychotherapist for the past 18 months who she sees every two to three weeks and reports that this has assisted her to process her trauma and support her psychological well-being.
The mother raised the father’s mental health as a significant issue in this matter. She stated that she strongly believes the father has an undiagnosed mental health condition. She stated that the paternal grandfather has a diagnosis of bipolar disorder and she reports that the father has demonstrated similar symptoms over the years she has known him. The mother describes the father as having different personalities and reported that the children have even asked her about the father’s two different personalities. The mother stated that the father’s mental health impairs his memory, stating that he will recall information wrong[ly], or describe situations as fact when they are actually untrue.
Further to this, the mother stated that the father experiences poor impulse control where she has observed significant changes in his personality. She stated that he will become fixated on something with such intensity and then quickly forget about this and “switch back”. She stated that the father presents as “different people” and reported that “you can see it in his eyes” and described how the father will undergo such a personality shift that even his morals and values change and he becomes increasingly paranoid. The mother stated that she worries for the children in the care of the father if he was to experience this personality change when they were in his care as they would not know what to do or how to keep themselves safe.
The father denied any mental health concerns but did state that he attended marriage counselling and saw a clinical psychologist under the Better Access to Mental Health Program “early on” in the break-up to “tick the boxes and see where I was at”. He stated that the marriage breakdown was quite stressful and conceded that he had at times “said stupid things” when asked if he had ever expressed suicidal ideation. He did not expand on this and stated that he no longer feels this way and denies current suicidal ideation.
The mother stated that the father had threatened suicide on numerous occasions throughout their relationship and had also threatened to burn down the maternal grandparents’ home with them in it, further adding to her concerns for the father’s mental health.
On 7 June 2024, Senior Judicial Registrar Glendenning made orders as follows:
PENDING FURTHER ORDER, THE COURT ORDERS THAT:
The children [X] born [in 2009] and [Y] born [in 2012] (“the children”) live with the Mother.
The children are to spend time with the father supervised by [EE Contact Centre] for not less than 2 hours each fortnight at such times and locations as directed by [EE Contact Centre].
No later than 4.00pm on 11 June 2024 the mother and the father must do all acts and things to initiate the intake process for [EE Contact Centre], and thereafter do all acts and things to expedite the completion of that process and the commencement of the children’s time with the father.
The mother and the father are to follow the reasonable directions of [EE Contact Centre] in respect to the supervised time the children are to spend with the father pursuant to these orders.
The father is to pay all costs associated with the supervision services provided by [EE Contact Centre] for the purposes of these orders.

6. The parties are permitted to provide a sealed copy of these orders to [EE Contact Centre].
Within 7 days of the date of these Orders, the mother and the father must do all acts and things necessary to enrol the children in [D Program] for family therapy.
Within 7 days of the date of these Orders, the father shall enrol in and then expeditiously complete the following:
a. An Anger Management program; and
A Men’s Behavioural Change program with [FF Family Services] (or like agency)
and shall provide details of each such enrolment and completion to the Independent Children’s Lawyer and to the mother.
The father is to provide a copy of the results from the Carbohydrate-Deficient Transferrin (CDT) testing he has undertaken to the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer within 48 hours of receiving same.
The father is restrained by injunction from consuming any alcohol for 24 hours prior to him spending time with the children.
The mother shall continue to attend upon her treating mental health provider and shall continue to follow all recommendations for her ongoing treatment.
The mother is permitted to provide a copy of the Child Impact Report dated 27 May 2024 to her treating mental health provider.
Except as otherwise permitted by these orders or by further leave granted by the Court, each party is restrained from the following:
a. Permitting either of the children to view any Court Document.

b. Discussing these proceedings with either of the children.
Causing or permitting any Court Document to be published on any media or social media service.
Causing or permitting any Court Document to be shown or given to any person that is not a party to these proceedings, a lawyer acting for a party to these proceedings, or the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
Denigrating the other party, their family or their friends to or in the presence or hearing of the children including via social media and will use their best endeavours to ensure that no one else does so and will remove the children from any environment when the same is occurring.
... the term “Court Document” means, for the purposes of this order, any order made in these proceedings, any expert report prepared for the purposes of these proceedings, and any document filed in these proceedings.

...

(As per the original)
The father spent supervised time with the children at EE Contact Centre on 26 June 2024, 14 and 27 July 2024, and 11 August 2024.
On 19 August 2024, Senior Judicial Registrar Glendenning made further interim orders (“the 2024 interim orders”). Those orders and notations provided for parental communication, some generic obligations, and otherwise included, relevantly, the following:
1. Order 2 of 7 June 2024 are [sic] discharged.
The children [X] born [in 2009] and [Y] born [in 2012] (“the children”) spend time with the Father supervised by [EE Contact Centre] for not less than 4 hours each fortnight, at such times and locations as directed by [EE Contact Centre], with orders 4 and 5 of 7 June 2024 to apply to the supervision of that time by [EE Contact Centre].
Upon the completion of three occasions of the children spending time with the Father pursuant to Order 2 herein, that order 2 is discharged.
Upon the discharge of order 2 herein pursuant to order 3 herein, the children thereafter spend time with the Father as agreed in writing between the parties and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, but in the absence of such agreement as follows:
From after school (or 3.30pm) each Wednesday to the commencement of school (or 9.00am) the following day, with the first occasion to commence on the Wednesday immediately following the final occasion the children spend time with the Father pursuant to order 2 herein; and

EVIDENCE

Statements of fact in these reasons are findings of fact on the balance of probabilities unless the context indicates otherwise.[30]
It is not necessary in these reasons for judgment to comment upon the entirety of the evidence including the evidence of each witness, nor to comment on every exhibit tendered.[31] Nor have I done so. However, every piece of evidence relied upon by the parties has been read and carefully considered by me.

Evidence of the father

The father was an honest and reliable witness. The father showed genuine concern for the child’s best interests. I accept the father’s evidence. I find the father was as described by the CCE, namely “genuinely concerned about the needs and well-being of the children”.

Evidence of the mother

The mother was an unreliable witness who was often evasive. She was at times dysregulated and had difficulties in answering the question asked of her. She loves the children and generally provides well for their physical needs. However, she is not possessed of insight into the children’s emotional and psychological needs and nor does she understand the requirement that the parties need to comply with orders of the Court.

Evidence of Ms G

Ms G was a reliable witness. I accept her evidence.

Evidence of Ms N

Ms N opined about matters on which she is not an expert and her evidence, in the totality of the evidence the Court was required to consider, did not materially assist the wife’s case.

Evidence of the Family Report writer, Dr M

Dr M completed the Family Report in this matter on 17 January 2025. Dr M gave insightful evidence that was of great assistance to the Court.
Dr M identified the following as issues in the proceeding:[32]
The mother’s lack of compliance with existing court orders and her unwillingness to comply with existing court orders;
ii. The history of confirmed family violence between the parents;
Concern regarding the mother’s well-being and emotional regulation based upon her presentation in the Family Report interviews;
Concerns regarding whether the mother’s received therapy has reduced or increased her trauma symptomatology;
Whether the children are influenced by the view of the mother regarding the father and if so, whether this constitutes psychological harm perpetrated by the mother;
The children’s described resistance to the father, which is challenged by the children’s love for the father;
The mother’s unwillingness to consider any time proposals between the children and the father, regardless of whether the Court determines the children are safe in the care of the father; and
Whether a change in residence in this matter would be beneficial for the children and allow them to have a relationship with both of their parents.
Dr M reported that the “overall impression of the father was that he is genuinely concerned about the needs and well-being of the children, due to his concerns in relation to the mother’s inability to support his relationship with the children”.[33]
Dr M noted that the mother presented as “extremely distressed and dysregulated” and said further of the mother’s presentation during the family report interview:[34]
The mother was asked about the impact on the children if they were to reside with the father, with the mother’s initial response being “traumatising” before she then moved onto her own issues, rather than the children’s issues, using “I” statements. The mother perceives she is a very child-focused parent and “I take parenting very seriously,” which appeared incongruous with her focus on herself at times during the interview.
And further:[35]
Ultimately, the mother’s presentation was of concern to the CCE throughout the interview, with the mother unable to contemplate the children having an ongoing relationship with the father in the future, even if the father completes programs or agrees to restraints, suggesting that the mother’s capacity to support the father’s ongoing relationship with the children is extremely limited.
Dr M raised concerns as to the mother’s ability to remain child-focussed, as follows:[36]
It is clear that these children continued a relationship with their father post separation, with the support and the consent of the mother, with the mother becoming aware of the father having another relationship appearing as central to the mother’s reasoning for cessation of the children’s time with the father, which if accurate, raises concerns about the mother’s capacity to be child focused.
Dr M said of the father as follows:[37]
The father’s willingness to do supervised time in the past and in the future with [X] and [Y] reveals the father’s willingness to respect the needs of the children. The father’s desire to attempt to repair his relationship with the children appears genuine and thoughtful.
Dr M summarised the matter as follows:[38]
This matter appears to be a complex matter, with concerns raised about the mother’s lack of compliance with existing court orders, the mother’s reasoning for cessation of time between the children and the father, the children’s exposure to the mother’s beliefs about the father, a history of confirmed family violence between the mother and the father and concerns about whether the mother has received appropriate treatment for her emotional regulation and well-being.

The mother’s lack of compliance with respect to Court orders remains an issue of concern, with the mother believing her lack of compliance is justifiable, with no anxiety or worry observed in the mother when discussing this issue. The mother’s lack of a solicitor is a significant issue in the matter, and it is believed she is not taking on the advice of solicitors she has had contact with. The mother did not appear concerned about potential court consequences for noncompliance with the existing Court orders and did not perceive consequences would apply in this particular matter. When examining matters in which a parent is non-compliant with Court orders, options for the Court to consider can be consequences for the non-compliant parent, such as financial consequences or a bond, involvement of third parties to assist with compliance of Court orders and commencement of therapy, including specific child and adult therapy.
In her evaluation of the family dynamics, when considering all the matters before her, Dr M concluded in addition to the above, relevantly, the following:
Concerns have been raised by the mother and the father about the other parent’s mental health issues in the current matter. Based upon their current presentation, there is no observable mental health issue evident for both parents, although the mother’s high level of emotional dysregulation in interview causes concerns for the CCE. The mother’s allegations of concerns regarding the father’s cognition, neurological issues and diagnosis of a major mental health disorder or a personality disorder does not appear consistent with the father’s presentation in interview, his empathy towards the mother and the children and his willingness to contemplate his own behaviour.
...
A strong theme in this intervention was [X’s] and [Y’s] strong, loyal and protective view regarding the mother. It is highly probable both children were exposed to the mother’s anxiety, distress and rumination post separation and post the mother finding out about the father’s relationship with [Ms G], leading to further confusion for the children and their potential rejection of the father. Both boys were able to articulate positive memories of the father, and both expressed love for their father in this assessment, suggesting that [X] and [Y] have some capacity to perceive their father from their own perspective. The mother believes she is not influencing the lens from which the children view the father, but examination of [EE Contact Centre] records and interviews with the children raises similarities about the information the children have been reporting to professionals and the beliefs espoused by the mother. The mother did demonstrate some insight into how her behaviour has influenced the children however, with the mother acknowledging the children have been acutely aware of her well-being and exposed to her rumination regarding the father.
It is thought these children blame the father for the mother’s observed distress and given their loyalty to the mother and the father’s documented family violence behaviour, it is easier for them to prefer the mother over the father. [Y’s] presentation was a concern to the CCE, with [Y] presented as highly protective and hypervigilant regarding the mother’s well-being and symptomatology, which causes him feelings of anxiousness and worry, which whilst the mother acknowledged, she appeared unaware of the impact her symptomatology is having on both children. Without additional psychological assistance and management, it is thought the mother’s symptomology will overwhelm the children’s psychological defences, resulting in ongoing psychological harm for the children. If the mother’s ability to protect the children from her emotions and worries is not able to change, it is strongly thought this will result in negative psychological outcomes for both [X] and [Y].

...
If the Court finds that the father does not pose an unacceptable or significant risk to the children, the Court may wish to consider whether the children’s rejection of the father is partly a result of the mother’s influence, and thus whether the mother has caused emotional and psychological harm to the children. Noting that the mother’s emotional dysregulation remains high, if this pattern of emotional harm for the children continues despite ongoing psychological therapy for the mother, there are concerns about the well-being of the children in the sole care of the mother and the children continuing to have an ongoing relationship with the father could provide the children with respite from the burdens of the mother’s emotional issues.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Parental responsibility is dealt with in Division 2 of Pt VII of the Act. Relevantly, sections 61CA and 61D of the Act provide as follows:
Consultation between parents on major long-term issues

If it is safe to do so, and subject to any court orders, the parents of a child who is not yet 18 are encouraged:
(a) to consult each other about major long-term issues in relation to the child; and

(b) in doing so, to have regard to the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.

...

Parenting orders and parental responsibility
(1) A parenting order confers parental responsibility for a child on a person, but only to the extent to which the order confers on the person duties, powers, responsibilities or authority in relation to the child.

(2) A parenting order in relation to a child does not take away or diminish any aspect of the parental responsibility of any person for the child except to the extent (if any):

(a) expressly provided for in the order; or

(b) necessary to give effect to the order.
(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long-term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision-making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues.
Pursuant to s 60CA of the Act:
In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, a court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.
To determine the best interests of the child, I must have regard to the relevant considerations in the Act as set out in ss 60CC(1), 60CC(2) and 60CC(2A) of the Act. Those sections provide relevantly:
How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

...
(1) ...in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2);
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)
Family violence is defined in s 4AB(1) of the Act as:
...violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member) or causes the family member to be fearful.
When considering the risk of family violence, the following is set out in s 60CG of the Act:
(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and

(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
In considering these matters, the Court must give consideration as to whether there is an unacceptable risk of family violence and/or an inability to promote the safety of a child and parent in the making of parenting orders, also involving a consideration of unacceptable risk. Upon establishing the existence of an unacceptable risk, the Court must then determine whether that risk “is able to be sufficiently managed or ameliorated”.[39]
The Court is required to assess its factual findings on the balance of probabilities, and the impact of those findings upon the assessment of future risk. Accordingly, Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) 65 Fam LR 288 is applicable. The Court’s determination as to what did or did not happen in the past is determined on the balance of probabilities. However, in hypothesising about future possibilities the Court is required to engage in an assessment of risk as a predictive exercise.[40] That assessment of risk is informed by the factual findings as to the past.

CONSIDERATION

The mother’s contraventions of the many time spent orders made have been intentional and remain so in respect of the November 2025 orders. She operates entirely of her own volition, outside the confines and requirements of legal orders. She is steadfast in her defiance, almost hysterical in her opposition. She sought to discharge the ICL in the context of the ICL suggesting that the children’s best interests would be served by them having an ongoing relationship with the father. She sought after the closure of the evidence at trial to file further applications seeking the discharge of the ICL which were not accepted for filing.
Following the making of the 2024 interim orders, the mother, mostly, failed to comply with them. She did not for example, comply with Orders 4(a), 4(b), 6(a), 6(b), 7, 10(a) and/or 12 of the 2024 interim orders. The mother did not comply with Order 4(b) on two occasions, being on 15 November 2024 and 29 November 2024. The mother refused to facilitate the changeovers for the children in accordance with Orders 6(a) and 6(b) at either their school or at Suburb HH McDonalds. The mother did not comply with Order 7 on more than one occasion by failing to pre-arrange with the children and with the school for the mother’s immediate attendance at the children’s school before, during and after the anticipated changeover times. On one occasion, being 3 October 2024, the mother left the children’s school with X while the father was waiting to for him in the playground to collect him. The mother caused the father’s mobile number to be blocked on the children’s mobile phones, and the father was unable to communicate with them by text or telephone them in accordance with Order 10(a) of the 2024 interim orders. The mother refused to communicate with the father through the agreed parenting communication application ‘AppClose’ in accordance with Order 12 of the 2024 interim orders.
The father completed a Carbohydrate-Deficient Transferrin test for alcohol during the proceedings which showed a reading within normal levels. The mother did not believe this was an accurate interpretation of the father’s alcohol use. The father was willing to agree to a restraining order regarding his abstention from alcohol use whilst the children were in his care to reassure the mother, but the mother remained intransigent, stating this would not reduce the risk factors as she perceives them.
Dr M observed that “the mother struggled to recognise positive experiences for the boys during the supervised time” and in response to questions by the report writer around that positive experience, the mother minimised the interactions between the father and child, saying by way of example ‘it went ok... playing with a ball’”.[41]
The supervision reports from MM Family Services of the father’s time with the child on 21 and 29 December 2025 and 5 January 2026, and the mother’s further evidence in her affidavit filed 9 February 2026, establish the mother’s ongoing inability to comply with orders of the Court and controlling behaviours as directed toward the victim father; her disdain for the father and child relationship; and her failure to support that relationship such that the child is compelled to eventually side with the mother in his rejection of his father.
The mother, in her affidavit filed 9 February 2026, used inflammatory descriptors (for example, “coercive pressure”),[42] to describe actions taken by the father that the father had a right to exercise at that time in respect of both children, given that the father had parental responsibility with respect to them. Whilst the mother made submissions on 17 March 2026, that the intended purpose of supervised visits as between Y and the father was to provide a protective environment for the child, that was not the Court’s intended purpose. The purpose was the recommencement of time as spent between Y and the father after a very long period, of approximately 14 months, during which no time spent had occurred, which, on the evidence before the Court, did not promote the child’s best interests. It was the Court’s determination that a structured environment would be beneficial to the child in the process of reacquainting the child with the father; that such an environment would, importantly, be likely to have the mother comply with orders of the Court (which ultimately did not occur); and that such a structure would make it easier for the child to be allowed to have a relationship with the father and further promote a move to unsupervised and overnight time, being the outcome supported by the facts of the case.
The mother’s evidence as contained in her affidavit filed 9 February 2026 was simply further evidence of the mother’s pattern of behaviour in not complying with orders of the Court.
In determining the child’s best interests as the paramount consideration, the Court must consider the following matters.

Section 60CC(2)(a) what arrangements would promote the children’s safety

The ICL submitted that the child was exposed to unacceptable psychological harm in the care of the mother, one part of that being the emotional pressure brought to bear on the child by the mother and his older brother X, X being in an enmeshed relationship with the mother. The father agreed that the safety of the child is not promoted in the care of the mother by virtue of the psychological harm caused to the child in the mother’s steadfast prevention of a relationship between the child and the father. Dr M opined that the mother, in, as I find, exposing the child to her views of the father (which were not consistent with his earlier experience) was perpetrating ongoing unacceptable psychological harm to the child. I find on the evidence that the mother has inflicted unacceptable psychological harm upon the child and that such harm continues. The Court finds the child is not so exposed in the care of the father. The mother conceded that the children were made aware of her emotions and trauma, and it is clear on the evidence that the mother’s approach is one of complete alienation of the children from the father. Although she cloaks this alienation of the children as an expression of the children’s independent wishes, it only commenced upon her rejection of the father completely, in the circumstances of his commencing a new relationship.

Section 60CC(2)(b) any views expressed by the child

The report writer said as to the views expressed by the child, at the time of the preparation of the family report, the following:[43]
...[Y] was asked to rate his resistance to spending time with the father on a zero to ten scale, with [Y] reporting he is a nine out of ten on the scale of resistance but a seven on a zero to ten scale for love for the father...[Y] was then asked about spending time with the father in a variety of ways, with [Y] ranking his anxiety at this out of ten. Spending time with the father at a local park was six out of ten for [Y] and a perceived seven out of ten for the mother; dinner in a public location was six out of ten for [Y] and a perceived eight out of ten for the mother and time with the father at his house was a nine out of ten for [Y] and a perceived ten out of ten for the mother.
And further that:[44]
[Y] reported he did not want to see the father again, and when asked why this was so, he stated because “how he used to be in the home and how he used to make me feel,” with [Y] describing the father as manipulative. [Y] was asked about returning to [EE Contact Centre], with [Y] asserting he could not do it yet and “only if I feel he has gotten better.”
The more recent views of the child, as expressed in early 2026 during a supervised visit, are contained in paragraph 92 above. The Court acknowledges the age of the child, being 13, and has considered the views as expressed by the child since separation and particularly since about May 2023, which have mostly been negative about his father. In contrast to that expression, has been the child’s expressed love for the father. That love however, does not have expression, free from the overbearing influence of the mother’s views. The child is unable to express his enjoyment of any time with the father. This is how he exists happily in the mother’s household. His now recollection of his time with the father when the parties were residing together, and in the period thereafter until May 2023, is distorted. That is not to say that he does not correctly recall his parents arguing and his father’s voice being frightening on occasion. The father concedes that he has a loud voice and that the children would have been frightened of him at times when the parties argued, or when he became situationally or otherwise aggressive. But that parental interaction and occasional outbursts did not result in the children not spending time and engaging with the father post-separation until May 2023. I accord little weight to the views of the child in the circumstances of the mother’s coercion of the children to align with her views.

Section 60CC(2)(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the children

The child likely suffers from dyslexia. He is supported in the learning environment of his school, and by his parents. Otherwise, his developmental, psychological, cultural and emotional needs are not being met in the mother’s household because of her poor mental health functioning, lack of proper medical treatment of it, and inability to see that her deprivation of the father/child relationship for the child is harming him as referred to otherwise in these sub-paragraphs.

Section 60CC(2)(d) the capacity of the parties to provide for the children’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

Dr M reported that “there is no observable mental health issue evident for both parents, although the mother’s high level of emotional dysregulation in interview causes concerns for the CCE”.[45]
Dr M opined of the mother in the family report as follows:[46]
The mother’s wish for the children to repair their relationship with the father appears well-intentioned, but there appears to be little context and weight behind the concerns of the mother, which raises concerns about the mother’s capacity to support the children’s relationship with the father in the future, particularly given her existing non-compliance with Court orders.

The concerns raised by the children to [EE Contact Centre] supervisor appears highly consistent with the concerns raised by the mother in these proceedings. If the mother has been exposing these children to her view of the father, which is not based or consistent with the children’s experience, this would be indicative of ongoing psychological harm.
Dr M said further, as follows:[47]
[Y] reported that the mother sends he and [X] to the maternal grandparents to manage her anxiety and regarded this as a “safe place.” [Y] reported that when the mother is doing an affidavit for Court, she tells the children to do other stuff but that the mother has to “relive it and it makes her feel sad and anxious and that makes me feel sad,” with [Y] noted to be crying and upset when discussing this. [Y] reported that when the mother is upset he gives her hugs, cooks dinner that night and reported that horse-riding helps the mother.
And, as follows:[48]
...the CCE informed the mother of [Y’s] wish to not complete an observation, noting that the mother had consented for the children to do an observation for the purpose of the Family Report in her interview with the CCE, with no worries or concerns expressed by the mother. Both the mother and [X] attempted to encourage [Y] to do the observation, with [Y] saying he did not want to. The mother then informed [Y] that if he has struggled to explain the reasons why he does not want to see the father in his interview, it would be best if [Y] can show the CCE his feelings about not wanting to spend time with the father during the observation, with [Y] responding saying, “I trust you mum. I will do the observation.” The mother appeared very proud of the reasoning she had provided to [Y] and expressed no worries about how she had articulated this.
It is clear on the evidence of the CCE that the father has a good capacity to provide for the needs of the child as referred to above; and that the mother does not. She has no or little capacity to be child-focussed. Whilst the mother has attended an extraordinary number of sessions with a psychotherapist, she has failed to attend upon a psychiatrist and/or psychologist in accordance with a mental health care plan as provided by her GP to address, and have medical treatment for, the various mental health issues from which she suffers.
The mother has included the child in discussions about the Court proceedings. Such behaviour does not promote his best interests and is in defiance of any Court orders addressing the matter.
In an email sent by the mother to the deputy principal of the children’s school on 24 November 2024, the mother said, “[t]he boys will not be complying with order tomorrow, I am supporting this and I will deal with the consequences”.[49] The mother prior thereto and after sending this email, continued to intentionally contravene the Court orders.
During cross-examination, the mother admitted that she has never told the child (or the children) that they must spend time with the father.

Section 60CC(2)(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so

It would be of great benefit to the child to have a relationship with both his parents. Both parents clearly love their children and, despite the child’s inability to show it (as forced upon him by his mother), the child loves each of his parents. There is no physical safety issue in the child spending time with either of his parents. There is, however, significant and unacceptable psychological harm to the child in the mother’s care as described above.

CONCLUSION

The Court finds there is an unacceptable risk of emotional and psychological harm to the child in the care of the mother and has considered how best that can be ameliorated in the context of the child’s age and his past limited time spent with the father by virtue of the mother’s actions. In that amelioration, there is a need to consider also the risks of destabilising the child (including in the context of his close relationship with his older brother), due to an actual and increased frequency of spend time arrangements between the child and the father. Any amelioration, however, must prioritise the importance of maintaining the child’s relationship with the father and promoting that relationship in the child’s best interests.
The father is primarily seeking a means by which the child is permitted to have a relationship with him and to spend regular time with him. The ICL determined, as joined by the father, that on the facts of the case, it was necessary to seek that the child live with the father and spend no time with the mother and his brother for a moratorium period of time, because they argue that any exposure of the child to the mother’s views and control, will result in no promotion of the child’s relationship with the father. Indeed, a blanket refusal for the child to have a relationship with the father.
The mother sought orders excluding the father from the child’s life save if the child expressed a view that he wished to spend time with the father. The mother will never promote nor accept the child’s expression of such a view, and the practical effect will be that the child is deprived of any experience of his father’s company, support and love. The mother proposed such orders in the context of her belief that the Court can do nothing else, and if contrary orders are made, she shall ignore them.
It is clear on the evidence that the orders as proposed by the father and the ICL are a possible outcome, but the practical effect of the operation of such orders may not achieve the outcome of a reunification of the child with his father, and hence the promotion of his best interests. Further, the effect will make difficult the important sibling relationship. Although I acknowledge that is already well developed. The evidence overwhelmingly supports that the father should have sole parental responsibility for long-term decision making concerning the child. It is not possible for the parties to act in any co-operative way. It is not possible for the mother to keep the father informed of matters which she should, and her behaviour is controlling. The father has the promotion of the child’s best interests as the foremost consideration. The mother does not. The child’s safety will be assured in the father having this responsibility solely.
The orders which the Court makes have the best opportunity for the promotion of the child’s best interests. There is a mechanism for the child to be placed in the care of the father in circumstances where the mother fails to comply with Court orders. There is an ability for the father to solely determine the place of schooling of the child in the event the mother’s interference in the child’s attendance at the school and collection from the school by the father, and her seeking of influence there, make it impossible for the orders as made by the Court to operate. The child can continue, subject to the mother’s compliance with the orders, his relationship with his brother and mother. If the mother cannot comply with these orders, then further orders may become necessary and more akin to those as sought by the ICL and the father. The general orders as to medical matters and the provision of the addresses of each of the parties to the other, and contact details, are ancillary parenting orders made necessary by the child living with each of his parents and there being a need for some limited co-ordination between them and provision of essential medical information.
